_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Columbia County, NY. Record survival from a county formed 1786 from Albany County. No major fire — excellent record survival. Unique sources include Livingston Manor rent rolls and Palatine German church records.
display_name: Columbia County, New York — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Claverack, then Hudson — frame and stone buildings
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved from Claverack to Hudson in 1805. No significant fire damage.
Served through the Civil War. Replaced by the current building.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Columbia County has not experienced a major courthouse fire. Records begin at the county's formation (1786); pre-1786 records for the area are under Albany County. Livingston Manor records are held separately by the New-York Historical Society and provide tenant documentation not available in courthouse records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Records survive. If {ancestor_name} was a tenant on the Livingston Manor, the courthouse may have limited records — check the Livingston family papers at the New-York Historical Society.
record_losses.source: Columbia County Clerk's Office. New York State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Good from county formation. Pre-1786 deeds under Albany County.
deeds.gaps: Livingston Manor leases are not in the county deed books — they are in the Livingston family papers.
deeds.repository: Columbia County Clerk's Office, Hudson; NY State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Columbia County Surrogate's Court; NY State Archives

marriages.repository: Town clerks; NY State Department of Health

court_minutes.repository: Columbia County Clerk's Office; NY State Archives

vital_records.repository: Town clerks; NY State Department of Health

substitute_sources.source_type: Livingston family papers and manor rent rolls
substitute_sources.description: The Livingston Manor records document tenants on one of the largest landholdings in colonial and early national New York. Rent rolls list heads of household, acreage, and payments.
substitute_sources.repository: New-York Historical Society; New York Public Library Manuscripts Division
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tenant families. The deed books will not contain these families because they did not own land.
substitute_sources.source: Kim, Sung Bok. Landlord and Tenant in Colonial New York. UNC Press, 1978.

substitute_sources.description: Lutheran and Reformed church records at Germantown document the 1710 Palatine settlement and its descendants. Records are in German through the late 18th century.
substitute_sources.repository: Evangelical Lutheran Church in America Archives; Reformed Church in America Archives; NY Genealogical and Biographical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Palatine families. Baptism records name parents and sponsors. Some records have been published by the German Genealogical Society of New York.
substitute_sources.source: Otterness, Philip. Becoming German. Cornell University Press, 2004.

substitute_sources.description: The Shaker community at New Lebanon maintained membership records, correspondence, and community journals from the 1780s through dissolution.
substitute_sources.repository: Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland; Library of Congress; Hancock Shaker Village archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying individuals who joined the Shakers. Membership records, admission books, and departure records track individuals entering and leaving the community.
substitute_sources.source: Andrews, Edward Deming. The People Called Shakers. Oxford University Press, 1953.

narrative_hooks: If {ancestor_name} was a Livingston Manor tenant, the courthouse deed books will not mention {pronoun_object} — check the Livingston family papers at the New-York Historical Society for rent rolls that documented every tenant family.

Palatine German church records at Germantown are in German and predate English-language civil records — {ancestor_name}'s baptism may be recorded in the language {pronoun_possessive} family spoke at home.
The Shaker community at New Lebanon kept meticulous records — if {ancestor_name} joined or left the Shakers, admission and departure books document the event.
Columbia County was formed from Albany County in 1786 — {ancestor_name}'s pre-1786 records are filed under Albany County.
source_references: Kim, Sung Bok. Landlord and Tenant in Colonial New York. University of North Carolina Press, 1978.
Otterness, Philip. Becoming German. Ithaca: Cornell University Press, 2004.
Andrews, Edward Deming. The People Called Shakers. New York: Oxford University Press, 1953.
New York State Archives. Guide to Records in the New York State Archives. Albany, 1993.
